topics.title: Collections Internal Working — HashMap, Comparators & Iterators
topics.description: Understanding how HashMap works internally (hash function → bucket → linked list → tree) is the most-asked Java collections interview topic. Comparable vs Comparator defines natural vs custom ordering. Fail-fast iterators (ArrayList, HashMap) throw ConcurrentModificationException on structural modification during iteration; fail-safe iterators (CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap) iterate over a snapshot.
topics.story: HashMap is like a library with 16 shelves (default buckets). Each book gets a shelf number from its title (hash). If two books land on the same shelf (collision), they chain together. When the library gets 75% full (load factor), it doubles shelf count and re-shelves everything (resize). Java 8 improved this: when a shelf gets more than 8 books, the chain becomes a balanced BST (treeification) for O(log n) worst case instead of O(n).
topics.analogy: HashMap bucket array = apartment building floors. hash() = elevator floor selector. Entry = apartment door. Collision = two tenants assigned same floor (chain/tree). Load factor 0.75 = 75% occupancy triggers building expansion. TreeMap = tenants sorted alphabetically on a single long corridor. LinkedHashMap = insertion-order guest book with fast lookup. Fail-fast iterator = library catalog that screams if a book moves mid-search.
topics.memoryAnchor: HashMap: default capacity 16, load factor 0.75, threshold = cap*LF → resize doubles. Collision: linked list, tree if >8 entries (TREEIFY_THRESHOLD). Java 8 key.hashCode() ^ (h>>>16) — spread high bits. HashMap not thread-safe; ConcurrentHashMap: segment locks (Java 7) → CAS + synchronized buckets (Java 8). Comparable: compareTo() on same class. Comparator: external, lambda-able. Fail-fast: ConcurrentModificationException on modCount change. Fail-safe: copies on write.
topics.firstPrinciples: A hash table trades space for O(1) average lookup. The hash function converts key to int; modulo bucket count maps to array index. Collisions are inevitable (pigeonhole); chaining (linked list) and open addressing handle them. A good hash function distributes keys uniformly — poor distribution causes all keys to land in one bucket (O(n) lookup).
topics.bruteForce: Without hashing: store key-value pairs in a list. Lookup = linear scan O(n). For N=1M entries, 1M comparisons per lookup. For sorted data TreeMap gives O(log n) but still slower than HashMap's O(1) average. HashMap wins for lookup-heavy workloads.
topics.optimizedApproach: HashMap: get/put O(1) average, O(log n) worst (treeified). TreeMap: O(log n) all ops, sorted. LinkedHashMap: O(1) with insertion order. ConcurrentHashMap: thread-safe, O(1) avg. Choosing right implementation: need sorted? TreeMap. need insertion order? LinkedHashMap. need thread safety? ConcurrentHashMap. need LRU? LinkedHashMap with removeEldestEntry.
topics.whenToUse: HashMap: most lookup/cache scenarios. TreeMap: sorted iteration, range queries (subMap, headMap). LinkedHashMap: LRU cache, ordered iteration. ConcurrentHashMap: shared state in multi-threaded apps. EnumMap: enum keys (array-backed, fastest). Comparable: natural ordering on own class (implement in model). Comparator: external/multiple orderings (pass to sort/TreeSet constructor).

examples.title: HashMap Internals — Hash, Collision, Resize
examples.explanation: hashCode() determines the bucket; equals() confirms key identity. Equal objects MUST have equal hashCodes — violating this breaks HashMap entirely. Java 8 treeifies buckets with >8 entries to prevent O(n) worst case from hash collisions. computeIfAbsent and merge are cleaner alternatives to manual containsKey + put patterns.
examples.realWorldUse: Entity ID as HashMap key: override hashCode/equals on @Id field only. Spring @Bean uses ConcurrentHashMap for singleton registry. Frequency counting (groupBy). Caching computed results. Graph adjacency list: Map<Node, List<Node>>.

examples.title: Comparable vs Comparator, Fail-fast vs Fail-safe
examples.explanation: Comparable puts ordering inside the class (only one natural order). Comparator externalises it (multiple orderings, reusable, lambda-friendly). Fail-fast iterators check modCount on every next() call — any structural modification changes modCount and triggers ConcurrentModificationException. CopyOnWriteArrayList creates a fresh array copy on every write, so iterators see a snapshot and never throw CME (at the cost of expensive writes).
examples.realWorldUse: TreeSet<Employee> uses Comparable. Priority queues with Comparator. Sorting REST list endpoints. CopyOnWriteArrayList for event listener lists (reads >> writes). ConcurrentHashMap for shared caches. Use removeIf/Iterator.remove() to safely remove during iteration.

problems.title: Group Anagrams
problems.description:
Group an array of strings by their anagram signature (sorted characters).

**Example:**
groupAnagrams(["eat","tea","tan","ate","nat","bat"])
=> [["eat","tea","ate"],["tan","nat"],["bat"]]

**Internals:** HashMap uses `hashCode()` + `equals()` for keys. Sorted string as key: all anagrams have the same sorted form.
problems.hint1: Two words are anagrams if their sorted char arrays are equal.
problems.hint2: Use a HashMap<String, List<String>> where key = sorted version of word.

problems.title: Top K Frequent Elements
problems.description:
Return the k most frequent elements using a min-heap of size k.

**Example:**
topKFrequent([1,1,1,2,2,3], k=2) => [1,2]

**Internals:** `PriorityQueue` is a binary min-heap. Maintain size k — if heap grows beyond k, poll the minimum. Remaining k elements are the top k.
problems.hint1: First build a frequency map with HashMap.
problems.hint2: Use a PriorityQueue (min-heap) of size k — keep only the k most frequent.
problems.hint3: When heap size > k, poll() to remove the least frequent.

problems.title: Sort Students by GPA then Name
problems.description:
Sort a list of students by GPA descending, then by name ascending for ties.

**Example:**
sort([Student(Alice,3.9), Student(Bob,3.7), Student(Charlie,3.9)])
=> [Alice(3.9), Charlie(3.9), Bob(3.7)]

**Key insight:** `Comparator.comparingDouble(Student::getGpa).reversed().thenComparing(Student::getName)` chains multiple sort keys.
